structured_payload_candidate.title: Use Proportional-Only Loops for Limiting Loops
structured_payload_candidate.equipment_scope: Limiting control loops (e.g., zone CO2 control loops)
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Use proportional only (P-only) loops for limiting loops to prevent integral windup from causing overshoot.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Use proportional only (P-only) loops for limiting loops (such as zone CO2 control loops, etc.).

evidence_quote: Use proportional only (P-only) loops for limiting loops (such as zone CO2 control loops, etc.).
evidence_text: Use proportional only (P-only) loops for limiting loops (such as zone CO2 control loops, etc.).
judge_reason: Application guidance from Section 5.1.8.1 on using P-only loops for limiting loops, with direct evidence.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Avoid Derivative Term in Control Loops
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Do not use the derivative term on any loops unless field tuning is not possible without it.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Do not use the derivative term on any loops unless field tuning is not possible without it.

evidence_quote: Do not use the derivative term on any loops unless field tuning is not possible without it.
evidence_text: Do not use the derivative term on any loops unless field tuning is not possible without it.
judge_reason: Guidance from Section 5.1.8.2 to avoid derivative term, supported by quote.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Control Loop Enable/Disable Based on System Status
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Control loops shall be enabled and disabled based on the status of the system being controlled to prevent windup.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: System status changes (e.g., system on/off).
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Enable or disable control loops accordingly.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Unless otherwise indicated, control loops shall be enabled and disabled based on the status of the system being controlled to prevent windup.

evidence_quote: Unless otherwise indicated, control loops shall be enabled and disabled based on the status of the system being controlled to prevent windup.
evidence_text: Unless otherwise indicated, control loops shall be enabled and disabled based on the status of the system being controlled to prevent windup.
judge_reason: Specific operational rule from ASHRAE Guideline 36 Section 5.1.2 about control loop enable/disable to prevent windup, with evidence quote from page 43.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Control Loop Initialization to Neutral Value
structured_payload_candidate.summary: When a control loop is enabled or reenabled, it and all its constituents (such as the proportional and integral terms) shall be set initially to a neutral value.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Control loop is enabled or reenabled.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Set loop and its constituents to a neutral value.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: When a control loop is enabled or reenabled, it and all its constituents (such as the proportional and integral terms) shall be set initially to a neutral value.

evidence_quote: When a control loop is enabled or reenabled, it and all its constituents (such as the proportional and integral terms) shall be set initially to a neutral value.
evidence_text: When a control loop is enabled or reenabled, it and all its constituents (such as the proportional and integral terms) shall be set initially to a neutral value.
judge_reason: Operational sequence from Section 5.1.3 on initializing control loops to neutral value, supported by evidence quote.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Neutral Control Loop Corresponds to Minimum Control Effect
structured_payload_candidate.summary: A control loop in neutral shall correspond to a condition that applies the minimum control effect, i.e., valves/dampers closed, VFDs at minimum speed, etc.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Control loop is in neutral state.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Apply minimum control effect (valves/dampers closed, VFDs at minimum speed).
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: A control loop in neutral shall correspond to a condition that applies the minimum control effect, i.e., valves/dampers closed, VFDs at minimum speed, etc.

evidence_quote: A control loop in neutral shall correspond to a condition that applies the minimum control effect, i.e., valves/dampers closed, VFDs at minimum speed, etc.
evidence_text: A control loop in neutral shall correspond to a condition that applies the minimum control effect, i.e., valves/dampers closed, VFDs at minimum speed, etc.
judge_reason: Specification from Section 5.1.4 defining neutral control loop as minimum control effect, with direct evidence.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Outdoor Air Temperature Sensor Selection
structured_payload_candidate.summary: When there are multiple outdoor air temperature sensors, the system shall use the valid sensor that most accurately represents the outdoor air conditions at the equipment being controlled.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Multiple outdoor air temperature sensors exist.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Use the valid sensor that most accurately represents outdoor air conditions at the equipment.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: When there are multiple outdoor air temperature sensors, the system shall use the valid sensor that most accurately represents the outdoor air conditions at the equipment being controlled.

evidence_quote: When there are multiple outdoor air temperature sensors, the system shall use the valid sensor that most accurately represents the outdoor air conditions at the equipment being controlled.
evidence_text: When there are multiple outdoor air temperature sensors, the system shall use the valid sensor that most accurately represents the outdoor air conditions at the equipment being controlled.
judge_reason: Clear operational guidance from Section 5.1.5 on selecting valid outdoor air temperature sensor, supported by quote.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Validity of Outdoor Air Temperature Sensors at Air-Handler Intakes
structured_payload_candidate.equipment_scope: Outdoor air temperature sensors at air-handler outdoor air intakes
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Outdoor air temperature sensors at air-handler outdoor air intakes shall be considered valid only when the supply fan is proven ON and the unit is in Occupied Mode or in any other mode with the economizer enabled.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Supply fan is proven ON and unit is in Occupied Mode or economizer enabled mode.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Consider the sensor valid.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Outdoor air temperature sensors at air-handler outdoor air intakes shall be considered valid only when the supply fan is proven ON and the unit is in Occupied Mode or in any other mode with the economizer enabled.

evidence_quote: Outdoor air temperature sensors at air-handler outdoor air intakes shall be considered valid only when the supply fan is proven ON and the unit is in Occupied Mode or in any other mode with the economizer enabled.
evidence_text: Outdoor air temperature sensors at air-handler outdoor air intakes shall be considered valid only when the supply fan is proven ON and the unit is in Occupied Mode or in any other mode with the economizer enabled.
judge_reason: Operational rule from Section 5.1.5.1 on validity conditions for OAT sensors at air-handler intakes, with evidence.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Outdoor Air Temperature Averaging for Global Sequences
structured_payload_candidate.summary: The outdoor air temperature used for optimum start, plant lockout, and other global sequences shall be the average of all valid sensor readings. If there are four or more valid outdoor air temperature sensors, discard the highest and lowest temperature readings.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Global sequences require outdoor air temperature.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Average all valid sensor readings; if four or more, discard highest and lowest before averaging.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: The outdoor air temperature used for optimum start, plant lockout, and other global sequences shall be the average of all valid sensor readings. If there are four or more valid outdoor air temperature sensors, discard the highest and lowest temperature readings.

evidence_quote: The outdoor air temperature used for optimum start, plant lockout, and other global sequences shall be the average of all valid sensor readings. If there are four or more valid outdoor air temperature sensors, discard the highest and lowest temperature readings.
evidence_text: The outdoor air temperature used for optimum start, plant lockout, and other global sequences shall be the average of all valid sensor readings. If there are four or more valid outdoor air temperature sensors, discard the highest and lowest temperature readings.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Adjustability of Setpoints, Timers, Deadbands, PID Gains
structured_payload_candidate.summary: All setpoints, timers, deadbands, PID gains, etc. listed in sequences shall be adjustable by the user with appropriate access level. Software points shall be used for these variables. Fixed scalar numbers shall not be embedded in programs except for physical constants and conversion factors.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: All setpoints, timers, deadbands, PID gains, etc. listed in sequences shall be adjustable by the user with appropriate access level whether indicated as adjustable in sequences or not. Software points shall be used for these variables. Fixed scalar numbers shall not be embedded in programs except for physical constants and conversion factors.

evidence_quote: All setpoints, timers, deadbands, PID gains, etc. listed in sequences shall be adjustable by the user with appropriate access level whether indicated as adjustable in sequences or not. structured_payload_candidate.title: Override Capability for All Points
structured_payload_candidate.equipment_scope: All points used in control sequences
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Values for all points, including real (hardware) points used in control sequences shall be capable of being overridden by the user with appropriate access level. If hardware design prevents this for hardware points, they shall be equated to a software point, and the software point shall be used in all sequences. Exceptions shall be made for machine or life safety.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Values for all points, including real (hardware) points used in control sequences shall be capable of being overridden by the user with appropriate access level (e.g., for testing and commissioning). If hardware design prevents this for hardware points, they shall be equated to a software point, and the software point shall be used in all sequences. Exceptions shall be made for machine or life safety.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Definition of 'Proven' Status
structured_payload_candidate.equipment_scope: All equipment with DI status point
structured_payload_candidate.summary: The term 'proven' (i.e., 'proven ON'/'proven OFF') shall mean that the equipment's DI status point matches the state set by the equipment's DO command point.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: The term “proven” (i.e., “proven ON”/“proven OFF”) shall mean that the equipment’s DI status point (where provided, e.g., current switch, DP switch, or VFD status) matches the state set by the equipment’s DO command point.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Definition of Software Point and Software Switch
structured_payload_candidate.summary: The term 'software point' shall mean an analog variable, and 'software switch' shall mean a digital (binary) variable, that are not associated with real I/O points. They shall be read/write capable (e.g., BACnet analog variable and binary variable).

structured_payload_candidate.title: Maximum Rate of Change for Control Loop Output
structured_payload_candidate.summary: The output of every control loop shall be capable of being limited by a user adjustable maximum rate of change, with a default of 25% per minute.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: To avoid abrupt changes in equipment operation, the output of every control loop shall be capable of being limited by a user adjustable maximum rate of change, with a default of 25% per minute.

evidence_quote: To avoid abrupt changes in equipment operation, the output of every control loop shall be capable of being limited by a user adjustable maximum rate of change, with a default of 25% per minute.

judge_reason: Parameter specification from Section 5.1.9 on maximum rate of change for control loop output, default 25%/min, with evidence.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Maintenance Mode Alarm Suppression
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Operators shall have the ability to put any device in/out of maintenance mode. All alarms associated with a device in maintenance mode will be suppressed except life safety alarms. If a device is in maintenance mode, issue a daily Level 3 alarm at a scheduled time indicating that the device is still in maintenance mode.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Device placed in maintenance mode.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Suppress alarms except life safety; issue daily Level 3 alarm reminder.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
Maintenance Mode. Operators shall have the ability to put any device (e.g., AHU) in/out of maintenance mode.
a. All alarms associated with a device in maintenance mode will be suppressed. Exception: Life safety alarms shall not be suppressed.
b. If a device is in maintenance mode, issue a daily Level 3 alarm at a scheduled time indicating that the device is still in maintenance mode.

evidence_quote:

structured_payload_candidate.title: Alarm Levels Definition
structured_payload_candidate.summary: There shall be 4 levels of alarm: Level 1: Life-safety message, Level 2: Critical equipment message, Level 3: Urgent message, Level 4: Normal message.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
There shall be 4 levels of alarm
a. Level 1: Life-safety message
b. Level 2: Critical equipment message
c. Level 3: Urgent message
d. Level 4: Normal message

structured_payload_candidate.title: Alarm Exit Hysteresis
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Each alarm shall have an adjustable time-based hysteresis (default: 5 seconds) to exit the alarm. Each analog alarm shall have an adjustable percent-of-limit-based hysteresis (default: 0% of the alarm threshold).
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
Each alarm shall have an adjustable time-based hysteresis (default: 5 seconds) to exit the alarm. Once set, the alarm does not return to normal until the alarm conditions have ceased for the duration of the hysteresis.
b. Each analog alarm shall have an adjustable percent-of-limit-based hysteresis (default: 0% of the alarm threshold, i.e., no hysteresis; alarm exits at the same value as the alarm threshold) the alarmed variable required to exit the alarm.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Minimum Speed Storage and Mismatch Alarm
structured_payload_candidate.summary: For each piece of equipment, the minimum speed shall be stored in a single software point. The active minimum speed parameter shall be read every 60 minutes via the drive's network interface. When a mismatch between the drive's active minimum speed and the minimum speed stored in the software point is detected, the minimum speed stored in the software point shall be written to the VFD via the network interface to restore the active minimum speed parameter to its default value, and generate a Level 4 alarm.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Mismatch detected between drive's active minimum speed and software point minimum speed.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Write software point minimum speed to VFD and generate Level 4 alarm.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: For each piece of equipment, the minimum speed shall be stored in a single software point; in the case of a hard-wired VFD interface, the minimum speed shall be the lowest speed command sent to the drive by the BAS. See Section 3.2.1.2 for minimum speed setpoints. The active minimum speed parameter shall be read every 60 minutes via the drive’s network interface. When a mismatch between the drive’s active minimum speed and the minimum speed stored in the software point is detected, the minimum speed stored in the software point shall be written to the VFD via the network interface to restore the active minimum speed parameter to its default value, and generate a Level 4 alarm.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Trim & Respond Set-Point Reset Logic Implementation
structured_payload_candidate.summary: T&R set-point reset logic and zone/system reset requests, where referenced in sequences, shall be implemented as described in Section 5.1.14.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Referenced in sequences.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Implement T&R logic as described.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: T&R set-point reset logic and zone/system reset requests, where referenced in sequences, shall be implemented as described below.

evidence_quote: T&R set-point reset logic and zone/system reset requests, where referenced in sequences, shall be implemented as described below.
evidence_text: T&R set-point reset logic and zone/system reset requests, where referenced in sequences, shall be implemented as described below.
judge_reason: Implementation requirement for Trim & Respond logic from Section 5.1.14.1, operational sequence, with evidence.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Trim & Respond Request Software Points
structured_payload_candidate.summary: For each downstream zone or system, and for each type of set-point reset request listed for the zone/system, provide software points: Importance-Multiplier (default=1), Request-Hours Accumulator, System Run-Hours Total, Cumulative%-Request-Hours. Request-Hours Accumulator and System Run-Hours Total are reset to zero automatically when System Run-Hours Total exceeds 400 hours or manually by a global operator command. A Level 4 alarm is generated if Importance-Multiplier > 0, Cumulative% Request Hours exceeds 70%, and total run hours exceeds 40.
configurable_values: Alarm threshold: Cumulative% Request Hours > 70% and run hours > 40
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
For each downstream zone or system, and for each type of set-point reset request listed for the zone/system, provide the following software points:
1. Importance-Multiplier (default = 1)
2. Request-Hours Accumulator. Provided SystemOK (see Section 5.1.19) is TRUE for the zone/system, every x minutes (default 5 minutes), add x divided by 60 times the current number of requests to this request-hours accumulator point.
3. System Run-Hours Total. This is the number of hours the zone/system has been operating in any mode other than Unoccupied Mode.
4. Cumulative%-Request-Hours. This is the zone/system Request-Hours divided by the zone/system run-hours (the hours in any mode other than Unoccupied Mode) since the last reset, expressed as a percentage.
5. The Request-Hours Accumulator and System Run-Hours Total are reset to zero as follows:
i. Reset automatically for an individual zone/system when the System Run-Hours Total exceeds 400 hours.
ii. Reset manually by a global operator command. This command will simultaneously reset the Request-Hours point for all zones served by the system.
6. A Level 4 alarm is generated if the zone Importance-Multiplier is greater than zero, the zone/system Cumulative% Request Hours exceeds 70%, and the total number of zone/system run hours exceeds 40.

evidence_quote: 5.1.14.2.  A “request ” is a call to reset a static pressure or temperature setpoint  generated by downstream
anchor_repair_reason: evidence_quote replaced with verbatim section heading line
evidence_text: 5.1.14.2.  A “request ” is a call to reset a static pressure or temperature setpoint  generated by downstream

structured_payload_candidate.title: Trim & Respond Setpoint Reset Logic
structured_payload_candidate.equipment_scope: Upstream system/plant setpoint controlled by T&R loop
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Trim & Respond logic shall reset the setpoint within the range SPmin to SPmax. When the associated device is OFF, the setpoint shall be SP0. The reset logic shall be active while the associated device is proven ON, starting Td after initial device start command. When active, every time step T, if R≤I, trim the setpoint by SPtrim. If there are more than I requests, respond by changing the setpoint by SPres*(R – I), but no more than SPres-max.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Associated device proven ON, after Td delay.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Every time step T: if R≤I, change setpoint by SPtrim; if R>I, change setpoint by (R-I)*SPres but no larger than SPres-max.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Trim & Respond logic shall reset the setpoint within the range SPmin to SPmax. When the associated device is OFF, the setpoint shall be SP0. The reset logic shall be active while the associated device is proven ON, starting Td after initial device start command. When active, every time step T, if R≤I, trim the setpoint by SPtrim. If there are more than I requests, respond by changing the setpoint by SPres *(R – I), (i.e., the number of requests minus the number of ignored requests) but no more than SPres-max.

evidence_quote: Trim & Respond logic shall reset the setpoint within the range SPmin to SPmax. When the associated device is OFF, the setpoint shall be SP0. The reset logic shall be active while the associated device is proven ON, starting Td after initial device start command. When active, every time step T, if R≤I, trim the setpoint by SPtrim. If there are more than I requests, respond by changing the setpoint by SPres *(R – I), (i.e., the number of requests minus the number of ignored requests) but no more than SPres-max.
evidence_text: Trim & Respond logic shall reset the setpoint within the range SPmin to SPmax. When the associated device is OFF, the setpoint shall be SP0. The reset logic shall be active while the associated device is proven ON, starting Td after initial device start command. When active, every time step T, if R≤I, trim the setpoint by SPtrim. If there are more than I requests, respond by changing the setpoint by SPres *(R – I), (i.e., the number of requests minus the number of ignored requests) but no more than SPres-max.
judge_reason: Operational sequence for Trim & Respond setpoint reset logic from Section 5.1.14.4, with evidence.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Trim & Respond Variables Definition
structured_payload_candidate.equipment_scope: Upstream system/plant setpoint controlled by T&R loop
structured_payload_candidate.summary: For each upstream system or plant setpoint being controlled by a T&R loop, define variables: Device, SP0, SPmin, SPmax, Td, T, I, R, SPtrim, SPres, SPres-max. Initial values are defined in system/plant sequences.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
For each upstream system or plant setpoint being controlled by a T&R loop, define the following variables. Initial values are defined in system/plant sequences below. Values for trim, respond, time step, etc. shall be tuned to provide stable control. See Table 5.1.14.3.
Table 5.1.14.3 Trim & Respond Variables
Variable Definition
Device Associated device (e.g., fan, pump)
SP0 Initial setpoint
SPmin Minimum setpoint
SPmax Maximum setpoint
Td Delay timer
T Time step
I Number of ignored requests
R Number of requests from zones/systems
SPtrim Trim amount
SPres Respond amount (must be opposite in sign to SPtrim)
SPres-max Maximum response per time interval (must be same sign as SPres)

evidence_quote:
For each upstream system or plant setpoint being controlled by a T&R loop, define the following variables. Initial values are defined in system/plant sequences below. Values for trim, respond, time step, etc. shall be tuned to provide stable control. See Table 5.1.14.3.
Table 5.1.14.3 Trim & Respond Variables
Variable Definition
Device Associated device (e.g., fan, pump)
SP0 Initial setpoint
SPmin Minimum setpoint
SPmax Maximum setpoint
Td Delay timer
T Time step
I Number of ignored requests
R Number of requests from zones/systems
SPtrim Trim amount
SPres Respond amount (must be opposite in sign to SPtrim)
SPres-max Maximum response per time interval (must be same sign as SPres)
evidence_text:
For each upstream system or plant setpoint being controlled by a T&R loop, define the following variables. Initial values are defined in system/plant sequences below. Values for trim, respond, time step, etc. shall be tuned to provide stable control. See Table 5.1.14.3.
Table 5.1.14.3 Trim & Respond Variables
Variable Definition
Device Associated device (e.g., fan, pump)
SP0 Initial setpoint
SPmin Minimum setpoint
SPmax Maximum setpoint
Td Delay timer
T Time step
I Number of ignored requests
R Number of requests from zones/systems
SPtrim Trim amount
SPres Respond amount (must be opposite in sign to SPtrim)
SPres-max Maximum response per time interval (must be same sign as SPres)
judge_reason: Definition of Trim & Respond variables from Section 5.1.14.3, a parameter spec, with evidence from table.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Lead/Lag Alternation Based on Staging Runtime
structured_payload_candidate.equipment_scope: Identical parallel staged equipment (e.g., CHW pumps, cooling towers)
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Unless otherwise noted, identical parallel staged equipment shall be lead/lag alternated when more than one is off or more than one is on so that the equipment with the most operating hours as determined by Staging Runtime is made the last stage equipment and the one with the least number of hours is made the lead stage equipment.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: More than one equipment off or more than one on.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Alternate lead/lag based on Staging Runtime: most hours = last stage, least hours = lead stage.

evidence_text: Unless otherwise noted, identical parallel staged equipment (such as CHW pumps and cooling towers) shall be lead/lag alternated when more than one is off or more than one is on so that the equipment with the most operating hours as determined by Staging Runtime is made the last stage equipment and the one with the least number of hours is made the lead stage equipment.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Runtime Points for Equipment Staging
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Two runtime points shall be defined for each equipment: Lifetime Runtime (cumulative runtime since equipment start-up, not readily resettable) and Staging Runtime (operator resettable runtime since last reset).
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
Two runtime points shall be defined for each equipment:
a. Lifetime Runtime: The cumulative runtime of the equipment since equipment start-up. This point shall not be readily resettable by operators.
b. Staging Runtime: An operator resettable runtime point that stores cumulative runtime since the last operator reset.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Faulted Equipment Definition and Alarm

If an operating equipment has any fault condition described subsequently, a Level 2 alarm shall be generated and a response shall be triggered as defined below.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Manual Staging Order Override
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Operators with appropriate access level shall be able to manually command staging order via software points, but not overriding the In-Alarm or Hand-Operation logic. Staging order changes initiated via operator override shall be instituted as part of normal staging events. Staging order shall remain overridden until released by operators.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Operator override command.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Change staging order as part of normal staging events; remain overridden until released.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
Operators with appropriate access level shall be able to manually command staging order via software points, but not overriding the In-Alarm or Hand-Operation logic in the following subsections.
1. Staging order changes initiated via operator override shall be instituted as part of normal staging events.
2. Staging order shall remain overridden until released by operators.

1. Staging order changes initiated via operator override shall be instituted as part of normal staging events.
2. Staging order shall remain overridden until released by operators.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Fault Response for Fans, Pumps, and Cooling Towers
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Upon identification of a fault condition: the next commanded off equipment in the staging order shall be commanded on while alarming equipment remains commanded on. If the next equipment fails to prove status, repeat until required quantity proven on or no more available. Set alarming equipment to last positions in staging order reverse chronologically. Staging order of non-alarming equipment shall follow even wear logic. Equipment in alarm shall run if called for by staging order and present stage.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Fault condition identified on operating equipment.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Command next off equipment on; if it fails, repeat; move alarming equipment to end of staging order; non-alarming equipment follow even wear; alarming equipment runs if called for.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
Upon identification of a fault condition:
i. For fans, pumps, and cooling towers:
(a) The next commanded off equipment in the staging order, Equipment “B”, shall be commanded on while alarming Equipment “A” remains commanded on.
(b) If Equipment “B” fails to prove status (i.e., it also goes into alarm), it shall remain commanded on and the preceding step shall be repeated until the quantity of equipment called for by the current stage has proven on, or there are no more available equipment.
(c) Set alarming equipment to the last positions in the lead/lag or lead/standby staging order sequenced reverse chronologically (i.e., the equipment that alarmed most recently is sent to last position).
(d) Staging order of non-alarming equipment shall follow the even wear logic. Equipment in alarm can only automatically move up on the staging order if another equipment goes into alarm.
(e) Equipment in alarm shall run if so called for by the lead/lag or lead/standby staging order and present stage.

evidence_quote:
Upon identification of a fault condition:
i. For fans, pumps, and cooling towers:
(a) The next commanded off equipment in the staging order, Equipment “B”, shall be commanded on while alarming Equipment “A” remains commanded on.
(b) If Equipment “B” fails to prove status (i.e., it also goes into alarm), it shall remain commanded on and the preceding step shall be repeated until the quantity of equipment called for by the current stage has proven on, or there are no more available equipment.
(c) Set alarming equipment to the last positions in the lead/lag or lead/standby staging order sequenced reverse chronologically (i.e., the equipment that alarmed most recently is sent to last position).
(d) Staging order of non-alarming equipment shall follow the even wear logic. Equipment in alarm can only automatically move up on the staging order if another equipment goes into alarm.
(e) Equipment in alarm shall run if so called for by the lead/lag or lead/standby staging order and present stage.
evidence_text:
Upon identification of a fault condition:
i. For fans, pumps, and cooling towers:
(a) The next commanded off equipment in the staging order, Equipment “B”, shall be commanded on while alarming Equipment “A” remains commanded on.
(b) If Equipment “B” fails to prove status (i.e., it also goes into alarm), it shall remain commanded on and the preceding step shall be repeated until the quantity of equipment called for by the current stage has proven on, or there are no more available equipment.
(c) Set alarming equipment to the last positions in the lead/lag or lead/standby staging order sequenced reverse chronologically (i.e., the equipment that alarmed most recently is sent to last position).
(d) Staging order of non-alarming equipment shall follow the even wear logic. Equipment in alarm can only automatically move up on the staging order if another equipment goes into alarm.
(e) Equipment in alarm shall run if so called for by the lead/lag or lead/standby staging order and present stage.
judge_reason: Operational fault response for fans, pumps, cooling towers from Section 5.1.15.5.b.2.i, with evidence.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Economizer High Limit Disable

Setpoints shall be automatically determined by the control sequences based on energy standard, climate zone, and economizer high-limit-control device type selected by the design engineer.

Setpoints shall be automatically determined by the control sequences (to ensure they are correct and meet code) based on energy standard, climate zone, and economizer high-limit-control device type selected by the design engineer in Section 3.1.4.3 or 3.1.6.2.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Damper/Valve Position Determination Methods
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Acceptable methods for determining position: analog actuator (position assumed equal to analog signal), floating actuator with position feedback AI, or floating actuator with position estimated by timing pulse commands with autozeroing in Unoccupied Mode (not acceptable for 24/7 applications).
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
The following are acceptable methods for determining position:
a. Analog actuator. Position may be assumed to be equal to analog signal to actuator.
b. Floating actuator. Provide either
1. Position feedback AI
2. Position estimated by timing pulse-open and pulse-closed commands with autozeroing whenever zone is in Unoccupied Mode and damper is driven full closed. This option is not acceptable for 24/7 applications.

evidence_quote: 5.1.18.2.  The following are acceptable methods for determining position:.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Hierarchical Alarm Suppression SystemOK Flag

structured_payload_candidate.title: SystemOK TRUE Conditions
structured_payload_candidate.summary: SystemOK shall be TRUE when all of the following are true: the system is proven ON, the system is achieving its temperature and/or pressure setpoint(s) for at least 5 minutes, and the system is ready and able to serve its load.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: System proven ON, achieving setpoint for 5 minutes, ready to serve load.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Set SystemOK to TRUE.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
SystemOK shall be TRUE when all of the following are true:
a. The system is proven ON.
b. The system is achieving its temperature and/or pressure setpoint(s) for at least 5 minutes
c. The system is ready and able to serve its load

judge_reason: SystemOK TRUE conditions from Section 5.1.19.3, operational sequence, with evidence.

structured_payload_candidate.title: SystemOK FALSE Conditions
structured_payload_candidate.summary: SystemOK shall be FALSE while the system is starting up (i.e., before reaching setpoint) or when enough of the system's components are unavailable (in alarm, disabled, or turned OFF) to disrupt the ability of the system to serve its load. This threshold shall be defined by the design engineer. By default, Level 1 through Level 3 component alarms shall inhibit SystemOK; Level 4 alarms shall not. The operator shall have the ability to individually determine which component alarms may or may not inhibit SystemOK.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: System starting up or insufficient components available.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Set SystemOK to FALSE.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
SystemOK shall be FALSE while the system is starting up (i.e., before reaching setpoint) or when enough of the system’s components are unavailable (in alarm, disabled, or turned OFF) to disrupt the ability of the system to serve its load. This threshold shall be defined by the design engineer for each system.
a. By default, Level 1 through Level 3 component alarms (indicating equipment failure) shall inhibit SystemOK. Level 4 component alarms (maintenance and energy efficiency alarms) shall not affect SystemOK.
b. The operator shall have the ability to individually determine which component alarms may or may not inhibit SystemOK.

evidence_quote:
SystemOK shall be FALSE while the system is starting up (i.e., before reaching setpoint) or when enough of the system’s components are unavailable (in alarm, disabled, or turned OFF) to disrupt the ability of the system to serve its load. This threshold shall be defined by the design engineer for each system.
a. By default, Level 1 through Level 3 component alarms (indicating equipment failure) shall inhibit SystemOK. Level 4 component alarms (maintenance and energy efficiency alarms) shall not affect SystemOK.
b. The operator shall have the ability to individually determine which component alarms may or may not inhibit SystemOK.

SystemOK shall be FALSE while the system is starting up (i.e., before reaching setpoint) or when enough of the system’s components are unavailable (in alarm, disabled, or turned OFF) to disrupt the ability of the system to serve its load. This threshold shall be defined by the design engineer for each system.
a. By default, Level 1 through Level 3 component alarms (indicating equipment failure) shall inhibit SystemOK. Level 4 component alarms (maintenance and energy efficiency alarms) shall not affect SystemOK.
b. The operator shall have the ability to individually determine which component alarms may or may not inhibit SystemOK.
judge_reason: SystemOK FALSE conditions from Section 5.1.19.4, operational sequence, with evidence.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Hierarchical Alarm Suppression Rules
structured_payload_candidate.summary: The BAS shall selectively suppress alarms for load components if SystemOK is FALSE for the source system that serves that load. If SystemOK is FALSE for a cooling water system, only high-temperature alarms from loads shall be suppressed. If SystemOK is FALSE for a heating water system, only low temperature alarms from loads shall be suppressed. If SystemOK is FALSE for an air-side system, all alarms from loads shall be suppressed.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: SystemOK FALSE for source system.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Suppress alarms on load components per rules.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
The BAS shall selectively suppress (i.e., fail to announce; alarms may still be logged to a database) alarms for load components if SystemOK is FALSE for the source system that serves that load.
a. If SystemOK is FALSE for a cooling water system (i.e., chiller, cooling tower, or associated pump), then only high-temperature alarms from the loads shall be suppressed.
b. If SystemOK is FALSE for a heating water system (i.e., boiler or associated pump), then only low temperature alarms from the loads shall be suppressed.
c. If SystemOK is FALSE for an air-side system (air handler, fan coil, VAV box, etc.), then all alarms from the loads shall be suppressed.

evidence_quote:
The BAS shall selectively suppress (i.e., fail to announce; alarms may still be logged to a database) alarms for load components if SystemOK is FALSE for the source system that serves that load.
a. If SystemOK is FALSE for a cooling water system (i.e., chiller, cooling tower, or associated pump), then only high-temperature alarms from the loads shall be suppressed.
b. If SystemOK is FALSE for a heating water system (i.e., boiler or associated pump), then only low temperature alarms from the loads shall be suppressed.
c. If SystemOK is FALSE for an air-side system (air handler, fan coil, VAV box, etc.), then all alarms from the loads shall be suppressed.
evidence_text:
The BAS shall selectively suppress (i.e., fail to announce; alarms may still be logged to a database) alarms for load components if SystemOK is FALSE for the source system that serves that load.
a. If SystemOK is FALSE for a cooling water system (i.e., chiller, cooling tower, or associated pump), then only high-temperature alarms from the loads shall be suppressed.
b. If SystemOK is FALSE for a heating water system (i.e., boiler or associated pump), then only low temperature alarms from the loads shall be suppressed.
c. If SystemOK is FALSE for an air-side system (air handler, fan coil, VAV box, etc.), then all alarms from the loads shall be suppressed.
judge_reason: Hierarchical alarm suppression rules from Section 5.1.19.5, operational sequence, with evidence.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Cascading Hierarchical Alarm Suppression
structured_payload_candidate.summary: This hierarchical suppression shall cascade through multiple levels of load-source relationship such that alarms at downstream loads shall also be suppressed.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: SystemOK FALSE at any level.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Suppress alarms at all downstream loads.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: This hierarchical suppression shall cascade through multiple levels of load-source relationship such that alarms at downstream loads shall also be suppressed.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Alarms Never Suppressed by Hierarchical Suppression
structured_payload_candidate.summary: The following types of alarms will never be suppressed by this logic: life/safety and Level 1 alarms, failure-to-start alarms, failure-to-stop/hand alarms.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Never suppress these alarm types.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
The following types of alarms will never be suppressed by this logic:
a. Life/safety and Level 1 alarms
b. Failure-to-start alarms (i.e., equipment is commanded ON, but status point shows equipment to be OFF)
c. Failure-to-stop/hand alarms (i.e., equipment is commanded OFF, but status point shows equipment to be ON)

structured_payload_candidate.title: Time-Based Suppression Delay for Thermal Zone Temperature Alarms
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Calculate a time-delay period after any change in setpoint based on the difference between the controlled variable at the time of the change and the new setpoint. The default time delay period for thermal zone temperature alarms: 18 minutes per °C (10 minutes per °F) of difference but no longer than 120 minutes.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
Calculate a time-delay period after any change in setpoint based on the difference between the controlled variable (e.g., zone temperature) at the time of the change and the new setpoint. The default time delay period shall be as follows:
a. For thermal zone temperature alarms: 18 minutes per °C (10 minutes per °F) of difference but no longer than 120 minutes

structured_payload_candidate.title: Chiller Fault Conditions
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Chiller fault conditions include: safety shutdown alarm, manual shut off indicated by Local/Auto switch, status remains off 5 minutes after start command, CHW isolation valve not open 90 seconds after command (parallel), CHW isolation valve not closed 90 seconds after command (series), CW isolation valve not open 90 seconds after command, or for 10 minutes CHW return temperature at least 3°C above CHWST setpoint and delta-T across chiller less than 2°C.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Any of the listed conditions.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Generate Level 2 alarm.
structured_payload_candidate.fault_condition: Safety shutdown, manual shut off, status off 5 min after start, isolation valve position mismatch, or low delta-T with high return temperature.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
Chillers
(a) Safety shutdown alarm condition either through network or hardwired alarm contact
(b) Chiller is manually shut off as indicated by the status of the Local/Auto switch from chiller gateway, or
(c) Chiller status remains off 5 minutes after command to start (note: this condition only applies when a chiller first starts, i.e., once status is proven, then status is no longer used as a fault condition because status will come and go if chiller cycles on low load), or
(d) CHW isolation valve feedback indicates valve is not open 90 seconds after valve is commanded open, or
(e) CHW isolation valve feedback indicates valve is not closed 90 seconds after valve is commanded closed, or
(f) CW isolation valve feedback indicates valve is not open 90 seconds after valve is commanded open, or
(g) For 10 minutes, chilled water return temperature has been at least 3°C (5°F) above the CHWST setpoint, and delta-T across the chiller, as determined based on the difference between chilled water return temperature and chilled water supply temperature measured at the chiller (i.e., not common CHWST), has been less than 2°C (3°F).

evidence_quote: 5.1.15.5.b.1.ii  or a chilled water or condenser water pump  dedicated to that chiller is faulted per

structured_payload_candidate.title: Boiler Fault Conditions
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Boiler fault conditions include: safety shutdown alarm, HW isolation valve not open 90 seconds after command, or boiler leaving water temperature remains 8.3°C below setpoint for 15 minutes and delta-T across boiler less than 6°C.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Any of the listed conditions.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: Generate Level 2 alarm.
structured_payload_candidate.fault_condition: Safety shutdown, isolation valve position mismatch, or low delta-T with low leaving temperature.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote:
Boilers
(a) Safety shutdown alarm condition either through network or hardwired alarm contact, or
(b) HW isolation valve feedback indicates valve is not open 90 seconds after valve is commanded open, or
(c) If boiler leaving water temperature remains 8.3°C (15°F) below setpoint for 15 minutes and delta-T across the boiler, as determined based on the difference between hot water supply temperature and hot water return temperature measured at the boiler (i.e., not common HWST), has been less than 6°C (10°F).
